speaker: interviewer
text: The date is July 19th, 1996. The survivor is Henry Kreisel. Interviewer, Devorah Levine. City/state, New York, New York. Country, USA. Language, English. Date, July 19th, 1996. The survivor is Henry Kreisel. Interviewer, Devorah Levine, D-E-V-O-R-A-H L-E-V-I-N-E. City, New York. Country, United States. Language, English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Kreisel, K-R-E-I-S-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna, Austria in 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 26th, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you the oldest, the youngest in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the oldest. There's two of us. I have a younger sister named Gerda.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in 1930. She is a year and a half younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- I left there when I was 10 years old or 10 and 1/2 years old. And I do remember my parents. They were good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Names were-- my father's name was Michael. My mother's name was [? Minah. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your Father have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hebrew-- the Hebrew name is Mickha'el, the Hebrew name. And the-- and the name of my mother was Myshka. I think it's a Polish name, Polish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What years were they born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in-- in 1886. And my mother was born in 1895.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents had a-- a grocery store at one time. Then they had a haberdashery store. And then I guess a result of the depression, they-- the store went under. And my father worked for my uncle in a-- in my uncle's haberdashery store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- my parents were Orthodox, 'cause I remember going to shul with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what the community was like growing up as a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't describe in terms of community, 'cause I wasn't thinking in terms of community. My schedule was I went to a regular public school in the daytime and I went to a cheder, a Jewish school, after-- after the regular public school. So that, in addition to playing with other kids, took care of my-- my social life, and going to shul with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I experienced quite a bit of anti-Semitism. There was in Vienna-- there was various-- a lot of anti-Semitism before Hitler. Hitler didn't create anti-Semitism either in Germany or Austria or any other part of the world. He only took advantage of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give an example of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a kid, I remember the kids calling me Jew boy, before-- before-- before Hitler came in. And then we had-- we had-- they had classes, religious classes, or religious moments, whatever it was, in the school. And the Jewish children went out during that period. Another boy and myself went out of class, left the class during that period.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were only two Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this particular class, there were just two of us. And this was not a-- a Jewish neighborhood specifically. There were Jewish neighborhoods in Vienna. And-- and kids used to call you Jew boy. And sort of in a sense, you're a little bit-- I don't think they were beating up on me then, at that point, before Hitler came. But they sort of-- you know, it made a difference known, particularly around Christmas, when all that hoopla, Christmas hoopla, which was much more than here in America. The kids were dressed in red, like little devils or little red jumpers and things. They were skating on the streets and throwing snowballs. And so you got into more of a Christmas atmosphere in Vienna, more than over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel left out of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a sense, yes. Yes. In a sense, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you involved with? Was there Jewish activities, Jewish clubs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, after Hitler came, of course, I was just-- and then when Hitler came in '38, I was barely nine years old. So after Hitler came, the-- the children became more aggressive towards Jews. You could be beaten up in the street for being a Jew. And I remember trying to-- to stay in the adults that are walking to-- for protection. And sometimes it would work. And sometimes adults would pull away and wouldn't give a damn, would just let the other kids beat up on the Jewish children or assault the Jewish children, taunt and physically assault. And that was-- sort of went on, went on all the time. And-- and then in the beginning, we still went to regular school there, to the regular classes. And then they changed that and the Jews went to different schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how your parents explained this to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall them ever explaining it to me. It became quite apparent what was going on. I don't know whether they knew much more about it than I did. I don't know if anybody could explain it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was this new Jewish school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a Jewish school. It may have been a public school where they-- where they-- where they put the Jewish children in a public school or in different classes from the regular children, from the other children after a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you and your friends do in your spare time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, when you're a kid, 9 and 10 years old, you don't-- you don't think in terms of spare time on what you do. So later on, after Hitler came, I joined the-- a Jewish Zionist organization. I can't think of the name right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Hashomer Hadati.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hadati.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hashomer Hadati, which is part of the Mizrachi movement. And I also joined the Maccabi, which was an exercise-- a health sports club. So I was involved in these two organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things did you do with Hashomer Hadati? We had meetings and talked about things and we sang songs, you know. And just, you know-- just-- it was something like B'nei Akiva. It was just like you felt a sense of belonging, of being a Jew, Jewish in that group. Was it a positive experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yes. It was a positive experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can you tell me about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really that much. Not really that much, because I was a child like any other child, you know. I went to kindergarten and then to public school. And that was a cheder, kept me pretty busy, and just playing around with other kids. I had no specific meaningful activities, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a time that you realized that things were going wrong, that things weren't quite what they should have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, things changed dramatically, even though there was extensive anti-Semitism, considerable anti-Semitism in Austria, but it became even more-- more radical after Hitler moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there would be-- they had more-- there were more assaults on Jews. There were more-- things began to happen. Before long, in the park you could only sit on certain benches with Jew-- with Jew marked on it. And so we're more liable to be assaulted or-- verbally and-- or physically or both. And the other kids didn't want to play with you, the other German-- the non-Jewish kids. I remember the class mostly [INAUDIBLE] area were Catholic. And there was one Protestant boy in the class and he was ostracized by the Catholics.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he and I [INAUDIBLE] were friendly together. But as soon as after Hitler came in and I saw him once in the streets, he wouldn't talk to me. I said, why? He says, because you're a Jude. You're a Jew. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I asked him why, because he refused to talk before, so he just-- he couldn't explain it. I guess he felt that was the best thing for him to do, not to associate with Jewish children and maybe be put in that same category.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister's reaction at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister being a year and a half younger, she's just a little kid. And I guess she must have made similar observations. We never discussed it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, when you're a kid, you take things as they are. You don't have big psychobabble conferences, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Kristallnacht, that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Kristallnacht, in November 1938. And my-- my-- where we lived, we lived right next to the haberdashery store owned by my uncle. And next to the haberdashery store was what they called a [NON-ENGLISH], a place where they sold-- where my grandmother had a store, like a-- it was like a cafe where they sold wine, different kinds of wine. And people just sat there on tables and chairs and they drank wine. And so that was next to that. And my father was-- I think my uncle may have already left for the United States, but I know he wasn't there. But my father was helping my grandmother take the things from her store from her store into the basement, because they were looting and things like that. And he was doing that, up and down, carrying things to the basement. And then some police, German Austrian police came to arrest my father. And there were a lot of Austrian people standing around. Some of them I knew, had known us all the time, neighbors, and some I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took my father, the police, while he was doing this, just off the street. And-- and-- I I ran to my father that I can I go with you? And he said, no. And the Germans-- the Austrians standing around thought it was very funny and they were laughing. They found this whole scene very funny, or they got a little bit what they call in German there's a word called schadenfreude, pleasure from other people's pain, that means to be an enormous pleasure which people find when others are having-- are troubled or having pain.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe for two reasons, to see other people get it. And certainly at the other hand, it shows them that they are not getting it. They're not getting it, so they-- but they feel good about the other guy getting it. And they just stood around laughing and [INAUDIBLE]. They knew where he was going, you know, the adults. And then the [INAUDIBLE] and I was trying to hold onto my father and some policeman pulled me off. And-- and then they took him to the police station. And from there, he went to a concentration camp in Germany There was one called-- not Dachau, another one-- one in Germany. I can't think of the name of it. That's where they initially took Jews to the concentration camps. And he was there for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the last time you saw--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He came back. He came out of the concentration camp a few months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Vienna and back to the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there? From there, we still lived in the [NON-ENGLISH] Bezirk and-- which was-- which was not a Jewish neighborhood, so Jews stood out. And there were always people making noises and-- and knocking on the windows and things like that. And we were on the ground floor. And also, then after a while, I think Jews had to move out of this neighborhood into Jewish-- neighborhoods they reserved for Jews. So we moved out of that neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which neighborhood did you move into? We moved into a place-- a place called [NON-ENGLISH] Bezirk. Bezirk means district, so they had 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, no, the 20th district from the 1st district. We got an apartment there and moved the furniture over there. And I went to another school there. And I think that school-- that school was also all Jewish, a public school where only Jews attended. What year was this in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in maybe latter part of '38 or '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What about your extended family at the time? You mentioned that your uncle moved to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The situation was-- with my family was my-- my grandparents moved to-- from Galicia, which was at one time a province-- a province of Austria, and then came back to Poland. They moved from there to-- to the United States, to New York. But my-- my mother was one year old at the time. Her younger siblings were all born in the United States, and therefore were United States citizens. And therefore, they had the right to move to America immediately, whereas my mother and my father, they had to take a visa and wait 'til the-- the-- the number would come up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Parents returned from-- from America back to Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then they returned-- the grandparents returned to-- from America to Vienna. I think at that time my grandfather needed some kind of operation. And strangely enough, the-- the-- Vienna had a higher level of medicine than in the United States at that time, back around I guess the First World War period, before the First World War period.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when it came time-- the war, when things were going bad, the extended family left, your uncles--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- on my mother's side, those who were American citizens, they left. My mother had a brother and two sisters, and they and their families left for the United States. And my grandmother left for the United States with them. I guess she had an American citizenship. She lived in America for a number of years. But my mother-- but my father's side remained in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have any desire to bring the family to America at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. But you couldn't-- unless you were a citizen, you couldn't go to the United States. They had a quota system and there were visas issued, but only a quota for so many each month. So my parents were waiting 'til their quota number came up so they could use a visa to the United States. But I guess there were so many applicants, and I guess a reduction in visas allowed by the United States, that there were long waiting lines and a lot of people never got out. By the time my parents got the visa, I think around 1941, the war had already been declared, and you couldn't go through regular channels anymore. You couldn't go like through France or through Belgium or through England. The plan my parents had was they sent my sister and myself on what they call a kindertransport. United Kingdom had issued visas for 10,000 Jewish children. And so we were-- from Germany and Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1939. And the plan my parents had was that these things were really pretty rough. Things were really very dangerous. And they sent us on the kindertransport. Of course, parents don't like to send small children away. But they felt they had to to save our lives. But the plan was hopefully that if they got their visa, they would then go to England, and on the way to America pick us up from England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any sense, was it difficult for them to get you onto the kindertransport?

speaker: interviewee
text: We really didn't think in those terms, just kids. I mean, you don't think in adult terms. Is it difficult, is it not difficult, what is the number system, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure it was difficult. It was a limited number, 10,000 altogether. I guess a lot of people who never got the kids on, who just stayed and they died. They were-- were exterminated there, you know? So you mentioned that you moved into a Jewish neighborhood in about 1938, '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. They moved us into, because you-- we had to move out of-- they wanted to concentrate Jews in certain neighborhoods.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you there before moving onto--

speaker: interviewee
text: A number of months. I don't exactly know. A number of months, because I went to school there. And all the kids in that school-- this was a public school, but all the kids in that school the Jewish. And they focused the Jews in certain neighborhoods. And I don't know if at that time they already had in mind what was happening. But I think they were-- the beginnings were already there. And the situations progressed with time. I don't even know if the Nazis themselves knew what ultimate fate they had in mind for the Jews, or if they themselves had been mentally and emotionally prepared for such drastic criminal activities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But nonetheless, then you had to have certain ration books for Jews and you could only shop at certain stores. You could only sit in certain benches in parks. And things began to sort of-- my father was sent to concentration camp. It was more Buchenwald. The name of the concentration camp was Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was sent to Buchenwald and then a few months later he came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came back. He came back. He looked quite-- he looked haggard and had a real hard time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he discuss what went on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not-- not with the children. Maybe with my mother and other adults, but not with two little kids at nine-- and nine and eight years old, nine and-- you know. You know. I guess they tried to protect-- the people tried to protect their children from the-- what was then the reality of the situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember spending time alone just with your mother with those few months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my father was in concentration camp naturally. We were there with my mother. We went to school. We kept on going to school. And I went and belonged to these two Jewish organizations. And that kept me pretty busy. And I guess the rest of the time, I was at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So until the kindertransport, you were in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in school until the kindertransport, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date of the kindertransport?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe I left around the 1st of September 1939. I believe I was the last transport out of Vienna. Anybody scheduled for the next one didn't make it, so I was very fortunate in this-- this extent. My sister left-- left Vienna on the transport in the beginning of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the separation from your parents, saying goodbye to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's sort of-- it wasn't-- it was a separation. It wasn't a separation, because we'll pick you up in a couple of months, you know. So it's sort of like-- you follow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a goodbye and then you'll see them a couple months later. That was the-- that was the plan. That was the hope. And that's what they told us, so we didn't really see it as a separation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel with people that you knew, with friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know anybody on-- on this particular kindertransport. I had seen-- maybe seen one or two people before. But looking out on the train station, I saw another kid whom I'd gone to class with. And-- and I talked to him. I waved to him. And subsequent-- and he also showed up. And I don't know. He-- so he-- I don't know what happened to him, but I saw him on the railroad station. And he had also plans for going on-- on the transport. But I don't know whether he-- since I was the last transport, I don't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to leave Vienna to--

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a long time. It was only a matter of the railroad station going from there to Holland. But because of the troop movements, which were-- the-- the war started on the 1st of September '39 with Poland. And there was a considerable amount of troop movement, so what would have been a turn maybe of 10, 12 hours perhaps, or less, took a couple of days, because they were shunting our train to a side while they let the troop trains go ahead. And then we went to Le Havre, France, a port. And we took the ferry across to England, to Dover, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the troops try to stop you? Were they involved at all? They didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. N. The troops weren't personally involved. The-- they weren't sort of like-- I don't think they were even antagonistic to us in any way. I-- I-- all that thing in Germany, it grew. I don't think essentially that the German people per se were much more evil any other people. It's just when you have an evil ideology, like Nazism, fascist Nazism or Marxist atheist communism, it's that evil ideology which leads you to evil thoughts, which in turn lead you to evil action. And you become evil in the process. But most of the troops there, they were sort of like an adventure, you know. They're going to go to the front. They're going to a war. And you know, like young kid-- mostly young children, young, maybe 18, 19, or whatever their ages were. And maybe even-- one of the-- I remember one of the group leaders, he was talking to them, you know. And they were talking for-- back to him, you know, so that there was no-- we didn't see any-- I didn't feel any antagonism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that what you were doing was an adventure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe partially, you know. Going on a rail-- I'm going on a train and going on a ferry, you know. And so in a sense, partially, yes. I wasn't scared. There were all the other people there. And you know, it was sort of like-- it was not like people who went to concentration camps, who were on the way to be slaughtered. There-- that was a different atmosphere. People knew they were on the way to be slaughtered. The reactions of the people at the railroad stations was quite difference. Even the people working the railroad, even the clerks in the offices of the various railroads knew they were going to slaughter. I think this came out in the-- in the movement-- in the movie, the French movie, called where it isn't it wasn't just Mr. Hitler and two or three of his cronies who did it, but it was entire populations who were involved in this, which of course they now deny. It's M. Hitler and the 20 other guys that did it, but we-- we never knew about it. They never knew about it, even though they may have had jewelry and other things, which-- which their-- their sons and people from the out-- out in that area gave to them, to the parents and the girlfriends, which they were wearing, you know. But They Know nothing about these things. And they saw these Jews every day being transported, never coming back. But they knew nothing, you know. But they did know. But when I left for-- for England, it was a different atmosphere and a different situation. It was a regular train and regular, you know, carriages and regular carriages were as-- whereas the other ones transported went on cattle trucks and would be 100 pressed-- 100 pressed into 1 carriage. We sat on regular benches in the carriage, regular carriages.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been a day or two later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened on your arrival there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was in England, I was met by a family in London, by a family, Christian family--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Names were Mr. And Mrs. Bannister. They were country people. They lived in a little village in West Wellow, in Hampshire, West Wellow. And it was a very rural village. And there was no-- and they were just people out of the goodness of their hearts wanted to take in a child. Most of the children, a great many of them, were not taken in by Jewish families, but were taken in by non-Jewish families. Of course, it never occurred to my parents seeing what was going on, the reactions of non-Jews to Jews, than any non-Jewish family would take in a Jewish child, so they presumed there'd be Jewish people taking care of my sister and myself. But my sister had come about a month earlier, also to another-- to a non-Jewish family, a Christian family. And-- and I guess they talked to these people. Neither of these families had children of their own, and this was their chance to get a child, although-- so they talked to them. And I guess through them, they applied for me t-- they got my name to-- to go there. And so my sister was only a few--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister was in the same village that you arrived in.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10-- another village, another village 10 or 15 miles away. And I used to get to see her from time to time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they an older couple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? They were both older couples.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do on the village? What did you do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shortly after I got there in the village, I-- it might have even been less than a village, maybe. What do you call that place which-- hamlet. They may both have been hamlets rather than villages. Mine was more of a village than the other. Where my sister was was more of a hamlet. And I went to school. I got there in September. I started school in September, the regular public school. And I went with the other children to the public school. And as I was going to school with-- talking to children, you learn English very rapidly. And this was a very rural area. We had to walk to school four miles to school. So that was kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any reaction to you being a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not among those kids. They just-- I don't even know if they really knew what a Jew was. I don't think-- know if those kids had-- maybe there were a couple of people who had really experienced anybody being Jewish, you know, had that knowledge and experience. Maybe one or two of the teachers had.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't react to you, the teachers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. I don't recall any-- any of that specifically. And-- and these were good people. And they were very good, decent to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to just switch tapes and continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
